original_abstract: This article examines the cultures of revolutionary militancy that emerged in the public spaces of France, Italy, and West Germany during the "red decade" (1968–1979). It argues that while radical movements across these borders shared common strategic frameworks and discourses on "counter-violence" and "self-defense," their practical expressions of militancy diverged significantly along national lines. Through a transnational and comparative lens, the study identifies several domestic factors that shaped these different trajectories: the historical legacies of anti-fascist resistance, the specific intensity of local social and industrial conflicts, and the varying degrees of provocation from neo-fascist groups. Central to this divergence was the role of the state; the article demonstrates how domestic policing strategies directly conditioned the level of force adopted by protestors. In particular, the frequent use of lethal force by Italian police fostered a unique environment where the revolutionary left endorsed firearms, blurring the lines between mass protest and armed struggle. By incorporating the French experience—where robust militancy did not transition into systemic terrorism—the author challenges teleological narratives in terrorism studies, suggesting that the transformation of protestors into armed insurgents was a contingent process heavily influenced by the routine violence of state repression rather than ideology alone.
humanized_abstract: This article analyses the cultures of militant leftism developed in the public space of France, Italy and West Germany during the ‘red decade’ (1968–1979). While sharing identical strategic objectives and vocabulary concerning counter-violence and self-protection, the militant left in each country performed activism in a manner that was heavily conditioned by national dynamics. In the light of a transnational historical perspective and a comparative approach, this article attempts to elucidate how national specificities – such as past anti-fascist resistance, local levels of social and industrial struggle, and the degree of neo-fascist provocation – were linked to law-enforcement practices. It shows how the latter directly affected the degree of violence exercised by protesters. For example, the comparative evidence reveals that the frequent use of firearms by the Italian police led to a form of escalation in which the left not only armed itself for self-protection but gradually endorsed the use of armed actions in demonstrations, thus moving from the level of the mass protest to that of armed terrorism. Including the French case in the comparative analysis also complicates the terrorist studies’ common teleological approach that posits that activist movements inevitably turn into armed revolutionary militias because of the inescapable nature of the militant ideology and because terrorism is the only efficient mode of armed struggle. Rather than ideology, state violence explains why left-wing activism in France evolved into radicalism but not into terrorism. The French exception illustrates that the activation of the terrorist option was a fragile and historically contingent process, in which the general principle of the counter-violent culture of the left turned into a practice of armed struggle only when ordinary police actions implied and permitted the use of lethal force on the part of protesters.
